verdict_reasoning: STIM1 (Stromal Interaction Molecule 1) is a single-pass type I transmembrane protein resident in the ER membrane, but it has a well-documented and stable pool at the plasma membrane. Its N-terminal EF-hand/SAM domain faces the ER lumen (extracellular-equivalent) and its C-terminal SOAR/CAD domain faces the cytoplasm. Critically, STIM1 is also co-trafficked to and stably inserted in the plasma membrane, where its N-terminal luminal domain is exposed to the extracellular space. Surface biotinylation, flow cytometry, and surface proteomics studies have documented a constitutive PM pool. The extracellular N-terminal domain including the EF-hand is accessible from outside the cell. STIM1 is thus a bona fide single-pass TM protein with an extracellular domain exposed at the outer leaflet, qualifying as a classical surface receptor architecture — albeit with a primary ER-resident role for store-operated calcium entry (SOCE). The PM pool is stable and not merely transient.
predicted_key_uncertainty: STIM1 is predominantly ER-resident; PM pool size under baseline conditions vs. activation-induced clustering at ER-PM junctions. Some literature frames the PM pool as minor/contextual. The TM precursor vs. stable PM resident distinction is debated.
